10.1016/j.jmp.2024.102887
Models of Associative Learning are models that predict behavior given the presence of environmental stimuli ( Pearce & Bouton, 2001 ). These models include some that may be considered classical (e.g.,  Bush & Mosteller, 1951; Estes, 1973; Mackintosh, 1975; Pearce, 1994; Rescorla & Wagner, 1972 ) and others that may be considered new (e.g.,  Brandon et al., 2000; Honey et al., 2022; Kruschke, 2001; Nihei et al., 2023; Stout & Miller, 2007; Thorwart & Lachnit, 2020 ). Models are important both because of their ability to explain behavioral phenomena and because they stimulate research ( Perez et al., 2019; Rescorla, 1988; Soto et al., 2023; Wasserman & Miller, 1997 ).

Concretely, these models attempt to explain how a given “input” stimulus may come to be associated to another “outcome” stimulus ( Miguez et al., 2014; Miller & Escobar, 2002; Wasserman & Miller, 1997 ). In a classical setting, the input stimulus is a biological irrelevant stimulus, such as a light or a tone, and called a Conditioned Stimulus (CS) while the outcome stimulus is a biological relevant one, such as food or a mild electric shock, and called an Unconditioned Stimulus (US). If the CS is a novel stimulus animals seem to have no reaction to it, but if the CS is presented together with the US it may eventually start to elicit the same response as the US (for example, salivation in the case of food, and fear responses in the case of the shock; see  Domjan & Delamater, 2023 ). Hence, it may be said that an association has been formed between the CS and the US, and the strength of this association may be inferred from the level of response to the CS (e.g.,  Rescorla & Wagner, 1972 ).

To explain these types of phenomena, models often assume that the intensity of the response elicited by the CS is an increasing function of the “associative value” between the CS and the US (e.g.,  Rescorla & Wagner, 1972 ). In turn, this associative value is a proposed mathematical quantity which would represent how much the organism has come to associate the CS with the US. Then, models normally try to (1) indicate how associative values are to be computed for any CS (or a combination of them) at any given time and (2) explain how these associative values may change after training ( Wasserman & Miller, 1997 ).

In this context, the Inhibited Elements Model (IEM henceforth) is a model of associative learning proposed by Brandon, Wagner, and Vogel ( Brandon et al., 2000; Wagner & Brandon, 2000 ) as a modification of Rescorla and Wagner’s (RW henceforth;  Rescorla & Wagner, 1972; Wagner & Rescorla, 1972 ) influential and successful model ( Soto et al., 2023 ). Concretely, IEM assumes the same learning rules as RW but it changes the way a compound of two or more stimuli should be represented when computing associative values. Firstly, while in RW it is assumed that the presentation of  
                         
                            A 
                            B 
                         
                       activates the (complete) representation of both  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                      , IEM assumes that the compounding of stimulus  
                         A 
                       and stimulus  
                         B 
                       activates only partially the representations given to  
                         A 
                       and to  
                         B 
                       when presented by themselves. Secondly, IEM assumes that the “salience” (i.e., number of elements in the representation) of all stimuli is constant.

This modification of the rules for the representation of compounds allows IEM to explain some discrimination tasks such as Saavedra’s biconditional discrimination (i.e., the consecutive and iterated training of  
                         
                            A 
                            Y 
                            + 
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            A 
                            X 
                            − 
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            B 
                            Y 
                            − 
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            B 
                            X 
                            + 
                         
                      ;  Saavedra, 1975 ) that cannot be explained by RW ( Brandon et al., 2000; Wagner & Brandon, 2000 ). Indeed, to explain this type of phenomenon a model should allow for compounds (i.e., a combination of discrete CS’s) to have different associative values than the sum of the associative values of its constituent stimuli (e.g.,  Wasserman & Miller, 1997 ).

The ability to differentiate between a compound and the sum of its parts is closely related to the classification of models as either “elemental” or “configural”. For this discussion, an elemental model may be understood as one where a compound stimulus acquires associative value through its constituent stimuli, while a configural model is one where each compound acquires associative value directly (see Section  2 ). While RW may be the most well-known elemental model, Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ) is perhaps the most influential configural model.

The distinction between configural and elemental has often been thought as a fundamental one, akin to the question of whether the whole is equal to the sum of its parts ( Wasserman & Miller, 1997 ). However, this distinction has been blurred by a recent work of  Ghirlanda (2015)  which has shown that it is always possible to construct an elemental model that reproduces Pearce’s configural model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ). Nonetheless, it should be noted that this construction is a mathematical device which may not be psychologically relevant.

Hence, the value of IEM goes beyond its ability to explain discrimination tasks since, according to Wagner and Brandon, IEM is “an elemental model that is generally isomorphic to Pearce’s (1987) configural model” ( Wagner & Brandon, 2000  p.30). Having an elemental model derived from psychological principles such as IEM reproduce the results of Pearce’s configural model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ) is of great theoretical significance since it would further blur the distinction between elemental and configural models.

The basic premise of IEM described above is complemented by  Wagner and Brandon (2000)  with the specification that all elemental stimuli within a compound should have the same number of inhibited elements, and with the principle that “the subset of theoretical elements representing stimulus  
                         i 
                       that is active when  
                         i 
                       is in compound with one stimulus,  
                         j 
                      , is statistically independent of the subset that is active when  
                         i 
                       is in compound with another stimulus,  
                         k 
                      ”(( Wagner & Brandon, 2000 )[p.30]). According to the example provided by  Wagner and Brandon (2000)  and  Brandon et al. (2000)  in which it is shown how IEM may account for biconditional discrimination, this “statistical independence” rule is meant to guarantee that the proportion of common elements between  
                         
                            A 
                            B 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            A 
                            C 
                         
                       equals  
                         
                            1 
                            / 
                            4 
                         
                      . This proportion is precisely the “similarity” measured by Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ) for these two compounds, and so the claim that IEM may reproduce Pearce’s models seems to have grounds.

Although it may look as if the two specifications above are enough to characterize IEM, they are in fact at odds with each other. Indeed, as I discuss below, it is not really possible for the proportion of common elements between  
                         
                            A 
                            B 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            A 
                            C 
                         
                       to be  
                         
                            1 
                            / 
                            4 
                         
                       and  at the same time  assume that in the compound  
                         
                            A 
                            B 
                            C 
                         
                       each stimulus has the same number of inhibited elements (at least if one also assumes, as Brandon, Wagner, and Vogel seem to do, that the inhibitory properties of one stimulus over another are independent of any other stimulus present in the compound). Then, the claim that IEM may reproduce Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ) is in doubt, and it seems the rule of “statistical independence” should be dropped.

Suppose then only the rule that each elemental stimulus within a compound should have the same number of inhibited elements is retained. To make meaningful predictions and compare IEM to other models, it is still necessary to somehow determine the proportion of common elements between different compounds.  Thorwart and Lachnit (2020)  have solved this problem by developing a recursive method to compute the proportion of elements of a stimulus that are inhibited by the others in a compound which, in turn, allows for the computation of the aforementioned proportion.

While the method proposed by  Thorwart and Lachnit (2020)  allows a full implementation of IEM by means of simulations, it leaves open the question of whether IEM does reproduce Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ). At least partially, the reason for this may be that IEM, being an elemental model, is not easily compared to non-elemental models such as Pearce’s model. The authors also leave open the problem of finding explicit formulas for the implementation of IEM, and the question of whether the number  
                         D 
                       of all stimuli that could be used in the experimental situation (which is used in the recursive formulas) plays or not a role in the predictions of IEM. Since learning should not be affected by the number of theoretical stimuli that may be added in the future, it is important to show that the parameter  
                         D 
                       has no effect on the predictions of the model (something that the authors conjecture from the simulations). Finally, the method by Thorwart and Lachnit does not explicitly say how associative values should be computed (which would be desirable) and utilizes an adapted version of RW’s error correction rule which makes the comparison of IEM with other models harder to attempt.

In these short notes I seek to advance the mathematical formulation of IEM initiated by  Thorwart and Lachnit (2020)  by providing an explicit formula for the proportion of elements of a stimulus that are inhibited by the others in a compound, and an explicit formula for the proportion of common elements between different compounds. In doing so I will be able to show that IEM does not reproduce the measure of “similarity” in Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ) unless the models are only applied to comparisons between compounds such that they either share no common stimuli or one of them contains all the stimuli in the other. Also, I will show that the predictions of IEM do not depend on the parameter  
                         D 
                      , and that (once made explicit) the computation of associative values and the application of the error correction rule coincide between Thorwart and Lachnit’s approach and the original approach as conceptualized by  Brandon et al. (2000) . Most of this work is done in Section  3 , and includes the consideration of a generalized version of IEM proposed by Thorwart and Lachnit which may interpolate between IEM and RW by the variation of a parameter.

In order to be able to compare IEM with the model proposed by  Pearce (1987, 1994)  and other models I will use the mathematical formalism of “Kernel Machines” as considered by  Ghirlanda (2015, 2018) . This formalism easily allows for the comparison of different models by proposing a unified way of conceptualizing them. Indeed, Ghirlanda has shown that many models of associative learning can be characterized as computing associative values  
                         
                            V 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                      , for any stimulus  
                         X 
                      , by means of the formula  
                         
                            
                               
                                  V 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∑ 
                                     
                                     
                                        Y 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        w 
                                     
                                     
                                        Y 
                                     
                                  
                                  k 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     , 
                                     Y 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                       where the sum is over all relevant training stimuli  
                         Y 
                       (including  
                         X 
                      ),  
                         
                            k 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               Y 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       may be considered a measure of “generalization” between  
                         X 
                       and  
                         Y 
                      , and  
                         
                            
                               w 
                            
                            
                               Y 
                            
                         
                       is a weight measuring the contribution from  
                         Y 
                      , through  
                         
                            k 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               Y 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                      , to the associative value of stimulus  
                         X 
                      . This formula is valid for all models considered by Ghirlanda, with different models characterized by how  
                         
                            
                               w 
                            
                            
                               Y 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            k 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               Y 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       are to be computed. In fact, for all models considered by Ghirlanda (and for IEM, as I will show) the computation of the weights  
                         
                            
                               w 
                            
                            
                               Y 
                            
                         
                       is virtually the same, so that only the computations of coefficients  
                         
                            k 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               Y 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       truly distinguishes models. Hence, it is possible to analyze abstractly all these models at once by means of the above formula, and then compare their specific predictions by utilizing their rules for the computations of  
                         
                            k 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               Y 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                      .

Since this formalism of Kernel Machines may not be widely known, I briefly describe it in Section  2  in the context of RW and Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ). Then, in Section  4 , I explicitly show how IEM (and the generalization proposed by  Thorwart and Lachnit (2020) ) may be cast as a Kernel Machine. Among other things, this formalization should help further compare IEM to other models cast in the same formalism. To appreciate the usefulness of Kernel Machines, let me remark that it is within this formalism that I will explicitly show under which conditions IEM and Pearce’s models may or not coincide in predicted associative values. In particular, I will show that when all stimuli are assumed of the same “salience” (as is done in the work by  Brandon et al. (2000)  for example) the models will coincide in predicted associative values exactly when the measure of “similarity” coincides (that is, when the relevant compounds either contain one another or share no common elements).

Finally, as an application of the results here developed, in Section  5  I will briefly analyze some learning phenomena by use of the formalism provided by Kernel Machines. Concretely, I will compare IEM (and Thorwart and Lachnit’s generalization of IEM;  Thorwart and Lachnit (2020) ), RW, and Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ) in the context of: (1) the effect of parameters on learning curves, (2) generalization decrements, (3) summation, (4) blocking, and (5) Saavedra’s biconditional discrimination ( Saavedra, 1975 ). Except for blocking, which is included because it is a phenomenon expected to be explained by any model of associative learning ( Miller & Escobar, 2002 ), all the other phenomena has been considered by  Thorwart and Lachnit (2020)  by means of simulations of IEM, RW, and Pearce’s model.

Ghirlanda (2015, 2018)  has shown that many models of associative learning can be described under the same mathematical formulation. This includes RW,  Pearce (1987, 1994)  configural model (P94 henceforth), and other major models such as the Replaced Elements Model ( Brandon et al., 2000; Wagner & Brandon, 2000 ).

All these models, and IEM, have been developed to explain and predict how training may change the “associative value”  
                         
                            V 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       of a certain training stimulus  
                         X 
                       (i.e., a CS) relative to a fixed outcome stimulus (i.e., an US). In particular, they propose rules for how  
                         
                            V 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       should be computed at any given time, and for how  
                         
                            V 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       should change under training.

Let  
                         T 
                       be the set of all training stimuli in an experimental situation. Then,  Ghirlanda (2015)  has verified that RW, P94, and others, can be characterized as computing  
                         
                            (1) 
                            
                               
                                  V 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     )

                                        ∑ 
                                     
                                     
                                        Y 
                                        ∈ 
                                        T

                                     X

                                     Y

                               w 
                            
                            
                               Y 
                            
                         
                       a weight,  
                         k 
                       a function that may be understood as a way of measuring “generalization” between stimulus  
                         X 
                       and  
                         Y 
                      , and where the sum is over all relevant stimuli  
                         Y 
                       in the training set  
                         T 
                      . Thus, for all these models  
                         
                            V 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       is computed as the result of adding up the weights  
                         
                            
                               w 
                            
                            
                               Y 
                            
                         
                      , but in proportions given by the computed “generalization”  
                         
                            k 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               Y 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       between  
                         X 
                       and  
                         Y 
                      . In particular, if the  
                         
                            
                               w 
                            
                            
                               Y

                            k 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               Y 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       should be computed is what differentiates one model from another ( Ghirlanda, 2015 ).

As for the changes in associative value upon training, all these models propose an “error correction rule” which can be described in all cases by the same equation. Indeed, let

The fact that this unified mathematical characterization is possible implies that these different models can be studied jointly and in a systematic way by comparing the predictions of the learning Eqs.  (1) and (2)  under the different choices of  
                         
                            k 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               Y 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       made by different models (a fact already used by  Ghirlanda (2018, 2022) ). This unified formalization of RW (an elemental model) and P94 (a configural model) shows that the distinction between elemental and configural models is not a fundamental one. In particular, it shows that it is possible for an elemental model, such as IEM, to reproduce Pearce’s configural model (see  Ghirlanda, 2015 ).

As mentioned in the introduction, the goal of this section is to show how to reproduce RW and P94 by applying Eqs.  (1) and (2)  with particular choices of  
                         k 
                       and of the relevant parameters. In what follows I will refer to models that can be formalized by means of Eqs.  (1) and (2)  as Kernel Machines, in accordance with the considerations by  Ghirlanda (2018)

On its most simple interpretation ( Rescorla & Wagner, 1972; Wagner & Rescorla, 1972 ) RW assumes that the associative value of a stimulus  
                            
                               X 
                               = 
                               A 
                               B 
                            
                         , which can be described as the compound of the “elemental” stimuli  
                            A 
                          and  
                            B

                                     V 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        A 
                                        B 
                                        )

                                     V

                                        A 
                                        )

                                     V 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        B 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     . 
                                  
                               
                            
                          That is, the associative value of a compound stimulus is the sum of the associative values of its constituent elements.

                                  1

                          and  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     λ 
                                  
                                  
                                     U 
                                     S 
                                  
                               
                               ≥ 
                               0 
                            
                         . The parameter  
                            
                               
                                  s 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          represents what is normally called the “salience” of the corresponding stimulus, and its role is to provide an adjustable parameter reflecting the intuition that different stimulus may acquire associative strength at different rates under equal training. Similarly,  
                            
                               
                                  b 
                               
                               
                                  U 
                                  S 
                               
                            
                          encodes how the specific US may affect the learning rate, while  
                            
                               
                                  λ 
                               
                               
                                  U 
                                  S 
                               
                            
                          represents the asymptotic level of associative strength that the US may support and it should be taken as  
                            0 
                          for training where the US is not present.

I now provide a way to cast this model as a Kernel Machine. Suppose a set of associative values

In summary, given a state of RW the above choices reproduce the associative values, and upon any training the update of associative values coincide, meaning that the formalization of RW as a Kernel Machine described here will always coincide with RW at any future time.

Remark 2 
                            The formalization of RW as a Kernel Machine I have just provided completely avoids the representation of stimuli as vectors, a representation commonly used (either explicitly or implicitly) to explain RW (e.g.,  Brandon et al., 2000; Ghirlanda, 2015; Thorwart & Lachnit, 2020 ). However, the kernel provided here exactly coincides with what is proposed by  Ghirlanda (2015, 2018)  by means of such an approach.

The formalization of RW as a Kernel Machine I have just provided completely avoids the representation of stimuli as vectors, a representation commonly used (either explicitly or implicitly) to explain RW (e.g.,  Brandon et al., 2000; Ghirlanda, 2015; Thorwart & Lachnit, 2020 ). However, the kernel provided here exactly coincides with what is proposed by  Ghirlanda (2015, 2018)  by means of such an approach.

As described by  Pearce (1994) , P94 proposes that any pattern  
                            A 
                          (i.e., either an “elemental” or “compound” stimulus) will have its associative value given by  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     V 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        A 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     E 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        A 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     + 
                                     
                                        
                                           ∑ 
                                        
                                        
                                           Y 
                                           ∈ 
                                           T 
                                        
                                     
                                     S 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        Y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     E 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        Y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          where  
                            
                               E 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          is the strength of the association between pattern  
                            X 
                          and the relevant US,  
                            
                               S 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  Y 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          is a measure of “similarity” between patterns  
                            X 
                          and  
                            Y 
                         , and the sum is all over all training stimuli. The similarity  
                            S 
                          can be characterized as  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        X 
                                        , 
                                        Y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       X 
                                                       ⋅ 
                                                       Y 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       ‖ 
                                                       X 
                                                       ‖ 
                                                       ‖ 
                                                       Y 
                                                       ‖ 
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          where  
                            
                               X 
                               , 
                               Y 
                            
                          are vector representations of the patterns in  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  N 
                               
                            
                          (which I have identified with the stimuli themselves) on the understanding that, given an order  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     u 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               … 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     u 
                                  
                                  
                                     N 
                                  
                               
                            
                          of “input units” which the patterns can activate, each pattern has a component along the standard basis vector  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        e 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               → 
                            
                          (of a given fixed magnitude) if and only if  
                            
                               
                                  u 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          is a feature of said pattern. This last rule may be cast alternatively as indicating that each relevant feature corresponds to one of the “elemental” stimuli  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               … 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                                  
                                     N 
                                  
                               
                            
                          represented as  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           σ 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           e 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  → 
                               
                            
                          with  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        σ 
                                     
                                     
                                        j 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          the fixed “activation level”, and so a compound  
                            X 
                          containing features  
                            
                               
                                  u 
                               
                               
                                  ρ 
                               
                            
                          with  
                            
                               ρ 
                               ∈ 
                               Γ 
                               ⊂ 
                               
                                  { 
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  … 
                                  , 
                                  N 
                                  } 
                               
                            
                          should be represented as  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     X 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           ∑ 
                                        
                                        
                                           ρ 
                                           ∈ 
                                           Γ 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 σ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ρ 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 e 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ρ 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        → 
                                     
                                     . 
                                  
                               
                            
                          Concretely, the above means that if  
                            
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               ⋯

                                           i 
                                        
                                        
                                           m 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               Y 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               ⋯ 
                               
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          share exactly the elemental stimuli  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           r 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               … 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           r 
                                        
                                        
                                           s 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          then  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        X 
                                        , 
                                        Y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       
                                                          
                                                             ∑ 
                                                          
                                                          
                                                             t 
                                                          
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          
                                                             σ 
                                                          
                                                          
                                                             
                                                                
                                                                   r 
                                                                
                                                                
                                                                   t 
                                                                
                                                             
                                                          
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                              
                                                 2 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       ∑ 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       t 
                                                    
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       σ 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       
                                                          
                                                             i 
                                                          
                                                          
                                                             t 
                                                          
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       ∑ 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       t 
                                                    
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       σ 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       
                                                          
                                                             j 
                                                          
                                                          
                                                             t 
                                                          
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     .

The above rule to compute associative values is complemented with the error correction rule  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     Δ 
                                     E 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        A 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           a 
                                        
                                        
                                           A 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           b 
                                        
                                        
                                           U 
                                           S 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              λ 
                                           
                                           
                                              U 
                                              S 
                                           
                                        
                                        − 
                                        V 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           A 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          where  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                                  
                                     A 
                                  
                               
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  [ 
                                  0 
                                  , 
                                  1 
                                  ] 
                               
                            
                          is parameter to adjust the rate of learning, and the remaining parameters have the same meaning as in RW.

Since  
                            
                               S 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  X
