                               V

                               2

                               V

                                  X

                               V

                         . Observe also that the asymptotic associative values of the reinforced compounds

                               Y

Note that the numbers in  Fig. 2  show predictions for the associative values of each elemental stimulus ranging from  
                            
                               1 
                               / 
                               4 
                            
                          to  
                            
                               1 
                               / 
                               3 
                            
                          maximal associative value, a difference that may be hard to assess empirically. They also show that the failure of RW in explaining the biconditional discrimination is not due to all elemental stimuli acquiring the same associative value, a conjecture that may be formed by the fact that all elemental stimuli are reinforced and non-reinforced the same number of times.

That Saavedra’s biconditional discrimination is solvable by test subject seems to be a robust finding (e.g.,  Lober & Lachnit, 2002 ) and so models should be able to predict it. This is the case for P94, IEM, and  
                            θ 
                         IEM, but not for RW. However, the issue still deserves further study as, for example, it has been shown that salience plays a role in the ability to solve the discrimination ( Byrom & Murphy, 2019 ), a phenomenon that has not been considered here.

Remark 21 
                            That  
                                  θ 
                               IEM becomes RW when  
                                  
                                     θ 
                                     = 
                                     0 
                                  
                                is true at any given computation of associative values if the weights (and parameters) coincide.                                      = 
                                     0

                                     0

                                  θ 
                               IEM.

Remark 22 
                            The asymptotic associative values discussed here are the associative values to which each stimulus approaches  at the end  of each block trail. Some preliminary simulations show that the evolution of associative values  within  each block of training has some interesting features and do not necessarily coincide with what is shown in  Fig. 2 .

The asymptotic associative values discussed here are the associative values to which each stimulus approaches  at the end  of each block trail. Some preliminary simulations show that the evolution of associative values  within  each block of training has some interesting features and do not necessarily coincide with what is shown in  Fig. 2 .

In this work I have shown the Inhibited Elements Model, and its generalization as proposed by  Thorwart and Lachnit (2020) , may be cast within the formalism of Kernel Machines studied by  Ghirlanda (2015, 2018) . In turn, this allows for meaningful comparison between these models and other major models (e.g.,  Pearce, 1987, 1994; Rescorla & Wagner, 1972 ). These comparisons are analytic in nature and provide explanations for phenomena observed in simulations.

The approach taken here by means of Kernel Machines has allowed to clarify the relationship between “similarity” (as discussed by  Brandon et al. (2000)  and  Wagner and Brandon (2000) ) and associative values. The approach has also shown that, except on particular cases and despite claims to the contrary by  Wagner and Brandon (2000) , the Inhibited Elements Model does not reproduce neither the similarity nor the associative values that are computed by the configural model proposed by  Pearce (1987, 1994) .

As an application of the theory developed here, I have contrasted the predictions of the Inhibited Elements Models with Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ) and the model by  Rescorla and Wagner (1972)  in the context of a few selected phenomena where equal salience for the elemental stimuli has been assumed. These applications have had the aim to provide an analytic counterpart to the simulations performed by  Thorwart and Lachnit (2020)  while at the same time connecting their work with the approach proposed by  Ghirlanda (2015, 2018, 2022) . I have also attempted to contrast the predictions with available data, and to point out where more data is needed. Note that the predictions have been obtained by means of either explicitly solving the equations of learning or by applying the method proposed by  Ghirlanda (2018)  to obtain expressions for target associative values in terms of other associative values.

Regarding the predictions of the different models, note that solving the equations of learning has shown that the Inhibited Elements Model, as does Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ), predicts that learning rates are independent of the number of stimuli in a compound. This is in contrast with Rescorla and Wagner’s model ( Rescorla & Wagner, 1972 ), as it predicts faster learning if the number of elemental stimuli in a compound increases. A systematic empirical study could try to assess which model better fits observed data.

The computations performed have also shown that all models here considered predict the same reduction in associative value after removing a stimulus from a trained compound. In contrast, adding a stimulus should have no effect according to  Rescorla and Wagner (1972) , and the same effect as removing one according to both Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ) and the Inhibited Elements Model. As there is evidence supporting both claims, further study is necessary to settle the issue.

The analysis of the phenomenon of summation has shown that the Inhibited Elements Model predicts no summation effect, while Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ) predicts a small amount of summation if the compounds share features, and  Rescorla and Wagner (1972)  always predicts full summation. The available data suggests a strong influence of modality on the phenomenon of summation, so each of these models may be advantageous depending on the situation.

Regarding blocking, all models considered here may be said to predict it, although the Inhibited Elements Model and Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ) predict a smaller effect than what is predicted by  Rescorla and Wagner (1972) . The fact that blocking may fail to be obtained under certain circumstances calls for a more detailed study of the effects of the parameters of learning on this phenomenon.

Lastly, the study of Saavedra’s biconditional discrimination ( Saavedra, 1975 ) has shown that the Inhibited Elements Model and Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ) can fully solve the discrimination, albeit with slightly different predictions for the asymptotic associative values of the elemental stimuli. In contrast,  Rescorla and Wagner (1972) , on the simple original interpretation used here, cannot account for the phenomenon. It has also been shown that the failure of Rescorla and Wagner’s model is not due to the elemental stimuli all acquiring the same associative value.

The analysis also has included  
                         θ 
                      IEM, the generalization of the Inhibited Elements Model proposed by  Thorwart and Lachnit (2020) , showing that in fact this model interpolates between the Inhibited Elements Model and the model by  Rescorla and Wagner (1972) . Moreover, the analysis has made explicit how the parameter  
                         θ 
                       connects these two models when applied to the phenomena here considered. However, note that this interpolation is guaranteed only at any fixed point in training, and care must be exercised when considering asymptotic associative values. Indeed, as the study of biconditional discrimination has shown, the asymptotic associative values predicted by Rescorla and Wagner’s model are not necessarily recovered from  
                         θ 
                      IEM simply by putting  
                         
                            θ 
                            = 
                            0 
                         
                      .

I hope the readers of this work may leave with two key notions. Firstly, that the formalization of the Inhibited Elements Model performed here has improved its understanding, by providing analytic predictions of the model and studying its internal properties, so those interested in modeling learning phenomena may consider using it if convenient. Secondly, that the formalism of Kernel Machines, which has been successful in blurring the distinction between elemental and configural models ( Ghirlanda, 2015 ) and useful in the study of the predictions of models ( Ghirlanda, 2018, 2022 ), has been extended here by the inclusion of the Inhibited Elements Model and by its application to more phenomena than those considered in the above works (i.e., to the study of learning curves, generalization, and biconditional discrimination).

In this sense, further work may attempt to enrich the theory by including more models within the formalism, by increasing the phenomena studied under it, or by attempting to use this approach to derive and test empirical predictions that may help judge the validity of different models. Some possible research subjects along these lines have been identified in this work.

I would also like to emphasize that the formalism of Kernel Machines developed by  Ghirlanda (2015, 2018)  has been crucial to the work performed here as it has the advantage of allowing for a unified approach to the analysis and comparison of different models. However, the approach also has other advantages, such as the fact that all computations can be carried out abstractly in terms of the “generalization coefficients”  
                         
                            k 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               Y 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                      , so that predictions are obtained from these computations by simply substituting the different values for the coefficients. Hence, the method can easily accommodate comparison where, for example, not all stimuli are assumed of the same salience, or where the generalization coefficient between compounds  
                         X 
                       and  
                         Y 
                       is different from that of  
                         X 
                       and  
                         Z 
                      , as this would simply mean specifying how the coefficients should differ. Even more, although it would require to be careful with the notation, it is even possible to admit that these coefficients are asymmetric between two stimuli  
                         X 
                       and  
                         Y 
                      , i.e., that  
                         
                            k 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               Y 
                               ) 
                            
                            ≠ 
                            k 
                            
                               ( 
                               Y 
                               , 
                               X 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                      . The models I have worked with here (and the ones formalized by  Ghirlanda (2015, 2018) ) have built-in the symmetry property  
                         
                            k 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               Y 
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            k 
                            
                               ( 
                               Y 
                               , 
                               X 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                      , but there are grounds to consider asymmetrical generalization coefficients. Indeed,  Inman and Pearce (2018)  have found asymmetry in a discrimination task for the same stimulus but with different magnitudes, which has led the authors to propose an asymmetric modification for the measure of “similarity” between stimuli proposed in Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ). Despite the asymmetry,  Ghirlanda (2022)  had no problem including the predictions of the model on a general analysis of the phenomenon of summation under the unified formalism used here.

Finally, it is my hope that this work has shown that the development of analytic formulas to study models and learning phenomena may help further their understanding. For example, the identification of necessary and sufficient conditions for the Inhibited Elements Model to reproduce Pearce’s model ( Pearce, 1987, 1994 ) has only been possible thanks to this analysis, as no amount (necessarily finite) of simulations could prove the general result obtained here. A similar remark holds for other conclusions reached in this paper, such as the proof that the total number of stimuli  
                         D 
                       plays no role on the predictions of the model, the identification of the role of the number of elements in a compound on its learning curve, that generalization decrements are a function only of the ratio of elemental stimuli between compounds, the explicit role of the parameter  
                         θ 
                       on the amount of summation or blocking predicted by  
                         θ 
                      IEM, or the explicit asymptotic values predicted by models under Saavedra’s biconditional discrimination ( Saavedra, 1975 ), among others.

I am grateful to Gonzalo Miguez for fruitful discussion and helpful comments regarding this work.

                         k

                            a

                               ] 
                            
                         
                      , whereas the second applies to RW.
